ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ham food product class; no specific honey-glazed baked ham subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers processed pig meat products including cured ham. No honey-specific glaze subcode exists; closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for honey baked ham as a food substance. Null used rather than invent a code.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of honey baked ham is approximately 85 g (2–3 thin slices), commonly served as a sandwich filling, cold cut platter component, or festive main.

nutrient_source.title: Honey baked ham — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06110015 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Foster-Powell et al. (2002); analogous estimate for cured/processed ham with honey glaze based on mixed protein-carbohydrate meat product category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for honey baked ham exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on processed meat products with added sugars/honey glaze. The carbohydrate content (7.27 g/100g) is modest; the medium GI reflects the honey glaze contribution. Glycemic load per 85 g serving = (58 × 7.27 × 0.85) / 100 ≈ 3.6 g, classified as low GL. Input record GI value of 58 (confidence 0.5) is retained and documented as an estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 900 mg per 100 g — substantially exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe processed foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit honey baked ham to occasional portions of no more than 30–50 g, as a single 85 g serving delivers approximately 765 mg sodium, representing over one-third of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Processed cured meat with high sodium and probable underreported sugars from honey glaze; processed meats independently associated with increased T2D risk per ADA guidelines
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should minimise consumption of honey baked ham: the high sodium content (900 mg/100 g) promotes insulin resistance, the honey glaze likely contributes sugars not fully captured in the 0 g/100 g sugar figure, and regular processed meat intake is associated with elevated T2D incidence per ADA 2024 Standards of Care.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, curing agents (sodium nitrite), and processed red meat classification — all independently linked to elevated cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Honey baked ham should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease, as its 900 mg/100 g sodium load, nitrite curing agents, and processed red meat classification are each independently associated with increased risk of hypertension, atherosclerosis, and major adverse cardiac events per AHA and WHO dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed cured pork with sodium nitrites and high arachidonic acid (omega-6) content promotes systemic inflammation and worsens RA joint swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid honey baked ham, as processed and cured meats are pro-inflammatory per EULAR dietary guidelines, and the 900 mg/100 g sodium content directly exacerbates RA-related joint oedema.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey baked ham is a processed pork product made from a cured pork leg that has been coated with a honey-based glaze and oven-baked until the exterior caramelises to a golden, slightly crisp finish. The curing process involves salt and sodium nitrite, which preserves the meat, imparts a characteristic pink colour, and contributes a savoury, mildly smoky flavour. The honey glaze adds sweetness and promotes Maillard browning during baking. The resulting texture is tender and moist with a subtly sweet, smoky, and savoury flavour profile. Honey baked ham is widely consumed in Western countries, particularly in the United States and the United Kingdom, as a festive centrepiece during Christmas and Easter, and is also popular as a cold cut for sandwiches and charcuterie boards. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 122 kcal, 17.93 g protein, 2.37 g fat, and 7.27 g carbohydrate. Its most clinically significant feature is its very high sodium content of 900 mg per 100 g, which places it firmly in the high-concern category for hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and type 2 diabetes management. The sugar value of 0 g/100 g reported in the HPB source is likely a data anomaly given the honey glaze; consumers and clinicians should consult product labels for actual sugar content. Honey baked ham is not suitable for halal or kosher diets. It is a high-protein, moderate-calorie food but should be consumed sparingly due to its sodium and processed meat classification.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in honey baked ham?
Can diabetics eat honey baked ham?
Is honey baked ham high in calories?
What is the protein content of honey baked ham?
Is honey baked ham suitable for hypertension?
How many calories in a slice of honey baked ham?
Is honey baked ham processed meat?
Can I eat honey baked ham on a low-sodium diet?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06110015, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). This is an approved primary regional source with an exact match for this food item. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence medium) documented with peer-reviewed basis.
notes: 1. sugar_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g in the HPB source. Given the honey glaze, this is likely a data anomaly or reflects the sugar content of the cured meat base only, excluding glaze contribution. This is confirmed zero per HPB source but flagged as potentially underreported; consumers should verify product labels. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source as this is a processed meat product with no plant fibre content (naturally zero for a pure meat product). 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific honey baked ham record (F06110015). USDA FoodData Central (NDB 07028 — Ham, honey, not canned) provides partial fatty acid data but was not used to override the HPB primary source to maintain source consistency; these fields remain -1 pending a dedicated fatty acid and micronutrient review. 3. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for honey baked ham exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. The estimate is based on the mixed protein-carbohydrate profile of processed meat with honey glaze, referencing Foster-Powell et al. (2002) AJCN international GI tables. 4. Halal status is false as pork is not halal; kosher status is false as pork is not kosher. 5. This food is classified as a processed meat (not ultra-processed) as it involves curing and baking but retains recognisable whole-muscle structure.
